role_summary: Mennonite minister and historian; co-drafter (with Andrew Young) of Martin Luther King Jr.'s April 4, 1967 Beyond Vietnam speech at Riverside Church; author of There Is a River: The Black Struggle for Freedom in America (1981); founder of the Veterans of Hope Project at Iliff School of Theology (1997); Distinguished Professor of Religion and Social Transformation at Iliff School of Theology.

photo.photographer: Veterans of Hope Project (the organization Harding co-founded)
photo.repository: Wikimedia Commons (via Veterans of Hope / Waging Nonviolence)

ai_reading: Harding is named in the Clarence B. Jones (entry 20) catalog page as the co-drafter (with Andrew Young) of King's April 4, 1967 Beyond Vietnam speech at Riverside Church. He is not himself a Civil Rights History Project interviewee. His standalone catalog page is added to anchor the Beyond Vietnam triangle (Harding + Young + Jones) the corpus's Jones page documents, and to give the broader cohort of SCLC-affiliated movement-historians a findable landing-place. The novel observation his page surfaces is the Mennonite-minister-and-historian crossover: Harding's career as both a movement participant (Mennonite House in Atlanta, 1961; SCLC speechwriting; Veterans of Hope) and a movement-historian (There Is a River, 1981, the canonical scholarly history of Black freedom struggle from the seventeenth century forward) is the catalog's rarest dual signature, the participant-as-historian whose 1981 book is itself a primary scholarly source many of the catalog's interviewees would have read.
biographical_paragraph: Harding was born July 25, 1931 in Harlem to Barbadian immigrant parents. He took a Bachelor's at City College of New York, a Master's in journalism at Columbia, a Master's in American history at the University of Chicago, and a doctorate in history at Chicago in 1965. After meeting Martin Luther King Jr. through the American Mennonite Brethren community in 1958, he and his wife Rosemarie Freeney Harding moved to Atlanta in 1961 and founded Mennonite House, a hospitality center for civil rights workers traveling the South. He worked alongside SCLC as a speechwriter and consultant; the April 4, 1967 "Beyond Vietnam" speech at Riverside Church, King's most controversial public stand against the Vietnam War, was substantially drafted by Harding and Andrew Young. After King's 1968 assassination Harding directed the Martin Luther King Memorial Center in Atlanta (1968-1970), then chaired the Department of History and Sociology at Spelman College. From 1981 his major scholarly work There Is a River: The Black Struggle for Freedom in America (Harcourt Brace Jovanovich) tracked the Black freedom struggle from the seventeenth century through the early twentieth, the first volume of a planned trilogy. In 1997 he founded the Veterans of Hope Project at Iliff School of Theology in Denver, where he taught as Distinguished Professor of Religion and Social Transformation until his retirement. He died May 19, 2014 in Philadelphia at eighty-two. Independent biographical sourcing in the catalog is the canonical scholarly source cited below [src: 1].

interview_snippets.speaker: Lisa Anderson Todd
interview_snippets.lead_in: Recalling the 1964 Freedom Summer volunteer orientation, where the training turned to nonviolence, Lisa Anderson Todd remembers Harding as one of the speakers:
interview_snippets.quote: I remember Vincent Harding being so impressive the way he speaks.

interview_snippets.speaker: Gwendolyn Zoharah Simmons
interview_snippets.lead_in: Gwendolyn Zoharah Simmons, who met Harding and his wife Rosemarie at the Mennonite House in Atlanta, describes being filmed for a Harding documentary project on movement workers who answered the call to organize:
interview_snippets.quote: But I found it interesting that Vincent Harding and his project where they filmed me and all and they called it answering the call.

interview_snippets.speaker: William Lamar Strickland
interview_snippets.lead_in: William Lamar Strickland, who worked with Harding at the Institute of the Black World in Atlanta, names him among the figures who shaped his own thinking and writing:
interview_snippets.quote: Well, what you understood, to go back to your earlier point, Vincent Harding was a tremendous influence.

interview_snippets.speaker: William Lamar Strickland
interview_snippets.lead_in: Strickland recounts how a television-history project the group had assembled became one of Harding's published works, alongside his teaching texts on movement history:
interview_snippets.quote: So Vincent Harding took what we had done and he published it as the other American Revolution. And then later he's done a book, he's done hope in history, how to teach the history of the movement, you know, to high school young people.

interview_snippets.speaker: William Lamar Strickland
interview_snippets.lead_in: Strickland places Harding in Atlanta in the months after the King assassination, recruited by Coretta Scott King to lead the new King center:
interview_snippets.quote: Vincent Harding invited to Atlanta after Martin was assassinated. And Coretta asked him to become director of the Martin Luther King Jr. Center for Nonviolent Social Change.

sources.title: Vincent Harding, There Is a River: The Black Struggle for Freedom in America
sources.publisher: Harcourt Brace Jovanovich, 1981 (reprinted Harvest Books, 1992); WorldCat OCLC 7178367
